method: Madhava series (India, ~1400 CE) — the original 'Leibniz' series
oneliner:
It sums the infinite alternating series π/4 = 1 − 1/3 + 1/5 − 1/7 + ⋯, derived from the arctangent power series evaluated at x = 1, converting a simple geometric ratio into π through endless addition and subtraction of unit fractions.
Madhava discovered it roughly 300 years before Leibniz and Gregory, meaning the series most people call "Leibniz" is a bit like calling America "Columbus's continent" — geographically flattering to the latecomer, historically embarrassing.

By inscribing and circumscribing regular polygons around a circle and doubling their sides repeatedly, Archimedes trapped π between ever-tightening upper and lower bounds, squeezing its true value from both sides.
Archimedes never had a computer, a calculator, or even decent notation for fractions — yet by sheer geometric stubbornness he cornered π between 223/71 and 22/7 using a 96-sided polygon, proving that infinity can be ambushed with patience and a compass.

It sums the infinite alternating series 1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7 + ⋯, exploiting the Taylor series of arctan(x) evaluated at x=1, since arctan(1) = π/4.
It's mathematically perfect and practically useless — after 300 terms you still don't have π correct to three decimal places, making it history's most elegant way to be spectacularly wrong for a very long time.

By inscribing regular polygons with doubling sides inside a circle, Liu Hui approximated π as the polygon's perimeter approaches the circle's circumference in the limit.
He essentially argued that infinity lives just around the corner — and kept doubling his way toward it, reaching 3.14159 with a 3072-gon while Europe was still debating whether zero was a number.

An infinite alternating series adds and subtracts fractions of the form 4/[n(n+1)(n+2)] starting at n=2 to the base value of 3, converging to π through cancellation of errors that shrink with each term.
Five centuries before calculus had a name in Europe, an Indian mathematician was already doing calculus — he just called it "finding π."

355/113 approximates π by taking the simple ratio of two integers that happens to land within 0.000008% of the true value, making it the most accurate fraction with a denominator under 10,000.
The mnemonic writes itself: cover the first three odd numbers in pairs — 1, 1, 3, 3, 5, 5 — split them into a fraction, and the universe hands you seven correct decimal places essentially for free.

Al-Kashi approximated π by inscribing and circumscribing regular polygons with 3·2ⁿ sides around a circle, computing their perimeters to squeeze π between ever-tightening bounds until reaching 16 correct decimal places.
He essentially out-Archimedes'd Archimedes by a millennium and a half, achieving with a 3×2²⁸-gon what no one had bothered to finish — proving that sometimes the best innovation is just refusing to stop iterating.

Randomly dropping a needle of length *l* onto a floor with parallel lines spaced *d* apart (where *l* ≤ *d*) yields a crossing probability of 2*l*/(πd), so counting hits over many throws lets you estimate π from pure geometry and chance.
A man in the 1700s realized you could compute a transcendental number by littering your floor with needles — making π the only mathematical constant you can approximate through an act that would annoy your houseguests.

It expresses π/2 as an infinite product of rational fractions, (2/1)·(2/3)·(4/3)·(4/5)·(6/5)·(6/7)···, where each successive pair of terms squeezes the value closer to π/2 by alternately overshooting and undershooting.
Wallis essentially convinced π to reveal itself through an infinite negotiation between even and odd integers — and remarkably, it works even though every single factor in the product is irrational's opposite: a perfectly boring rational number.
